groups.title: The databases
groups.sub: Structured, cited term-databases queried by scripts/research.py

items.work: RenaissanceMagicDB
MedievalMagicDB
TheosophicalAlchemyDB
Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic: Illicit Learned Magic in the Later Middle Ages and Renaissance
items.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

items.work: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
items.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

items.work: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels: Theurgic Ideas and Practices, 13th–16th Centuries
items.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

items.work: Dan Attrell & David Porreca (trans.), Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
items.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

items.work: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
items.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.

items.work: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
items.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

items.work: Sophie Page & Catherine Rider (eds.), The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
items.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

items.work: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon
items.abstract: A modern scholarly edition of a 17th-century handbook of magic (called a "grimoire") that lists methods and diagrams for summoning and commanding spirits, put together and corrected by Peterson from several old manuscript copies held in the British Library.

items.work: Claire Fanger, Rewriting Magic
items.abstract: A close study of a 14th-century French monk's own written account of creating a prayer ritual he believed could instantly grant knowledge — a project that got his book publicly condemned and burned as heresy in 1323. Fanger uses his firsthand story to explore how medieval people justified using something so close to forbidden magic.

items.work: Stephen Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements
items.abstract: Skinner's doctoral thesis traces specific magic tools and step-by-step methods (like ritual instruments and spirit-summoning procedures) as they passed from ancient Greek-Egyptian magic texts, through Byzantine Greek manuscripts, into later European handbooks of magic — showing one continuous tradition rather than separate inventions.

items.work: Frank Klaassen, Making Magic in Elizabethan England
items.abstract: A scholarly edition of two handwritten magic manuals from Elizabethan England: one aimed at elite, book-learned ritual magic, and one for everyday folk magic like treasure-hunting, healing, and protection charms. Together they show how magic changed during the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the early age of printing.

items.work: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Grimorium Verum
items.abstract: Peterson's edition and translation of a notorious 18th-century French handbook of magic that openly gives instructions for calling on demons — one of the few such books from its era that doesn't dress up its subject matter in euphemism.

items.work: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Key of Solomon
items.abstract: Peterson's corrected and annotated edition of the most famous Renaissance-era handbook of ceremonial magic, which gives instructions for making magical tools and performing rituals, based on comparison of multiple old manuscript copies.

items.work: Richard Kieckhefer, Forbidden Rites: A Necromancer's Manual of the Fifteenth Century
items.abstract: A historian publishes and translates a real 15th-century handbook of "necromancy" found in a German library. It isn't about raising corpses -- it's a working magician's manual with prayers, lists of spirits to summon, and step-by-step rituals for consecrating a magic book, giving a rare inside look at how educated people actually practiced ritual magic in the late Middle Ages.

items.work: Crowley et al., Illustrated Goetia
items.abstract: A modern, illustrated update of occultist Aleister Crowley's 1904 translation of a famous old demon-summoning manual known as the Goetia. Later editors add plain-language commentary and pictures of the 72 spirits so contemporary readers can understand a ritual system that originally assumed a lot of specialized background knowledge.

items.work: Fanger, 'Things Done Wisely by a Wise Enchanter'
items.abstract: A scholarly article on a strange medieval debate: could spoken words alone have real power? The author shows that some 13th-century thinkers, like Roger Bacon, argued certain words could work through natural (almost proto-scientific) causes, while others insisted that any word-magic that actually worked had to be secretly powered by demons.

items.work: John Michael Greer (trans.), De Imaginibus: Thābit ibn Qurra's Astral High Magic
items.abstract: A modern English translation of a 9th-century Arabic astrology text on how to make talismans -- charged objects meant to draw down the influence of planets and stars. The book explains, step by step, how ancient astrologers believed you could time and construct an object so it would carry a specific astrological power.

items.work: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), Book of Magic
items.abstract: This short title refers to Joseph H. Peterson's online edition of an Elizabethan-era manuscript spellbook (its full title is "Book of Magic, with Instructions for Invoking Spirits"). It's a real magician's notebook from the late 1500s, mixing prayers, magic circles, and drawings of spirits, angels, and demons within a Christian framework.

items.work: Joseph Peterson (ed.), grimoire editions
items.abstract: Not one single book, but Joseph Peterson's long-running project (the "Twilit Grotto: Esoteric Archives" site and related small-press editions) of transcribing and translating historic "grimoires" -- old handbooks of spells, symbols, and spirit-summoning instructions -- and making them freely readable online.

items.work: Peterson & Harms, Book of Magic with Instructions for Invoking Spirits
items.abstract: A modern transcription, by Joseph Peterson and Dan Harms, of a real handwritten spellbook from around 1577-1583, kept today at the Folger Shakespeare Library. It's an eclectic collection of spells and invocations with magic circles and drawn portraits of the spirits, angels, and demons it describes how to contact.

items.work: Richard Kieckhefer, 'The Specific Rationality of Medieval Magic'
items.abstract: An influential essay arguing that medieval magic wasn't just ignorant superstition -- it followed its own internal logic, blending pieces of religion, early natural science, and folk tradition into systems that made coherent sense to the people who used them.

items.work: Stephen Skinner & Daniel Clark (eds.), Ars Notoria
items.abstract: A scholarly edition of a medieval "study magic" manual that promised readers could master an entire school subject almost instantly, by praying while gazing at elaborate diagrams called notae -- with angels supposedly doing the actual teaching.

items.work: The Magic of Rogues
items.abstract: Using real 16th-century English court records, this book tells the true stories of two magic scandals: a group hunting for buried treasure with a spell that went wrong, and a magician swept into a treason case with his employer. It shows that everyday folk magic was usually punished lightly, not treated like the witch trials people imagine.

items.work: Wright & Klaassen, Everyday Magicians
items.abstract: Drawing on Tudor-era court records and surviving spellbooks, this book argues that most people practicing magic in 16th-century England were ordinary neighbors solving everyday problems -- money troubles, love, theft, sickness -- and were treated by the law as minor offenders, not the dangerous witches later legend made them out to be.

items.work: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
items.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

items.work: Frances Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
items.abstract: A classic study arguing that the Renaissance philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned at the stake in 1600, was driven less by early modern science than by "Hermeticism" -- a set of ancient-seeming magical and mystical teachings he believed held the secret key to controlling nature.

items.work: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons
items.abstract: Looks at how educated Europeans between the 1400s and 1700s wrote about witches and demons, and argues these ideas were not fringe nonsense but fit logically with how those same thinkers understood nature, religion, history, and politics at the time.

items.work: Deborah E. Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
items.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

items.work: Hans Dieter Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
items.abstract: A translated collection of real spells, charms, and magical recipes written on papyrus in Greco-Roman Egypt roughly 1,600 to 2,200 years ago, giving readers direct access to what ordinary magical practice actually looked like rather than what critics said about it.

items.work: György Szönyi, John Dee's Occultism
items.abstract: Argues that John Dee's wide-ranging interests in science, mathematics, and angel-summoning were all held together by one goal: his belief that humans could spiritually elevate themselves toward a god-like state through hidden knowledge and symbols.

items.work: Håkan Håkansson, Seeing the Word: John Dee and Renaissance Occultism
items.abstract: A doctoral thesis arguing that John Dee's strange mix of interests -- grammar, the Kabbalah, astronomy, alchemy, and math -- were not scattered hobbies but one connected project: recovering a lost perfect language Dee believed could unlock both nature's secrets and God's truth.

items.work: Paola Zambelli, White Magic, Black Magic in the European Renaissance
items.abstract: Examines Renaissance thinkers' theories about magic, focusing especially on how learned 'high' magic practiced by scholars overlapped and interacted with everyday popular magic and witchcraft accusations among ordinary people.

items.work: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
items.abstract: Traces how modern Wicca -- the pagan witchcraft religion that emerged in 20th-century England -- was actually assembled by blending older folklore, ceremonial magic, and Romantic ideas about nature, rather than being an unbroken survival of ancient paganism.

items.work: Glyn Parry, The Arch Conjuror of England: John Dee
items.abstract: A biography arguing that John Dee was not just an eccentric magician on the sidelines of history, but a real political operator whose magic and angel-conversations were tangled up with Elizabethan court intrigue and Europe's religious conflicts.

items.work: Hanegraaff, Forshaw & Pasi, Hermes Explains
items.abstract: A collection of short, accessible essays by thirty scholars answering common questions about 'Western esotericism' -- the broad academic label for astrology, alchemy, magic, and other hidden-knowledge traditions -- aimed at clearing up popular misconceptions about the field.

items.work: John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World
items.abstract: A translated collection of ancient Greek and Roman curse tablets -- small inscribed objects people buried or dedicated to gods to harm a rival, win a court case, or wreck a love affair -- showing how ordinary people used magic to handle everyday conflicts.

items.work: Norman Cohn, Europe's Inner Demons
items.abstract: Traces where the medieval stereotype of witches flying off to secret orgies with the devil actually came from, and shows that key 'evidence' used to justify witch hunts was based on invented stories and outright forgeries rather than real observed practices.

items.work: Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic from the Medieval Latin and Islamic World to Renaissance Europe
items.abstract: A collection of the author's essays tracing how astrology (reading the stars to predict events) passed from Islamic and Latin medieval scholars into the Renaissance, and how astrologers actually worked as court advisors and public intellectuals, not just armchair theorists.

items.work: Brian Copenhaver, Hermetica
items.abstract: A fresh English translation of the Hermetica, a set of mystical Greek and Latin texts from ancient Egypt that Renaissance readers mistakenly believed were far older wisdom from a legendary sage named Hermes Trismegistus, with an introduction placing the texts in their real historical setting.

items.work: Carlo Ginzburg, Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath
items.abstract: Ginzburg pulls together witch-trial records, old sermons, folklore, and popular artwork from across Europe to argue that stories of witches flying to night gatherings echo a much older, widespread folk belief in spirit-journeys (a kind of trance travel found in shamanic traditions), rather than being straightforward evidence of devil-worship.

items.work: Carlo Ginzburg, The Night Battles
items.abstract: Ginzburg reconstructs the beliefs of the benandanti ("good walkers"), a folk group in 16th-century northern Italy who said their spirits left their sleeping bodies at night to fight witches and protect the harvest, and traces how Church inquisitors gradually pressured them into confessing to being witches themselves.

items.work: Charles Zika, The Appearance of Witchcraft
items.abstract: Zika studies printed woodcuts and illustrated pamphlets from 16th-century Europe to show how artists invented and spread the now-familiar image of the witch (flying, naked, cooking over a cauldron), arguing these pictures shaped public fear of witchcraft as much as trial records or sermons did.

items.work: Ioan P. Couliano, Eros and Magic in the Renaissance
items.abstract: Couliano argues that Renaissance magic wasn't primitive superstition but an early, systematic theory of persuasion: thinkers like Ficino and Bruno believed a skilled operator could sway people's minds and emotions by understanding how desire and imagination work, making the Renaissance magician a distant ancestor of the modern advertiser or propagandist.

items.work: John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells
items.abstract: Gager collects and translates ancient Greek and Roman "curse tablets" -- thin sheets of lead or other metal inscribed with spells asking gods or spirits to harm a rival, a thief, or a romantic competitor -- with commentary explaining how ordinary people, not just professional sorcerers, used these rituals in everyday disputes.

items.work: Lamont, In Blood and Ashes: Curse Tablets and Binding Spells in Ancient Greece
items.abstract: Using newly published curse tablets alongside archaeological finds, Lamont shows that cursing rituals in ancient Greece were a common tool ordinary people -- tavern keepers, barbers, sailors -- used to settle everyday grudges and rivalries, not just a fringe practice of shady magicians.

items.work: Meyer & Mirecki, Magic and Ritual in the Ancient World
items.abstract: This edited collection of scholarly essays asks what counted as "magic" (as opposed to accepted religion) across the ancient Near East, Greece, Rome, Judaism, and early Christianity and Islam, using real spell texts, amulets, and rituals to explore where societies drew that line and why.

items.work: Brann, on the Fasciculus chemicus
items.abstract: This is a scholarly review by Noel Brann of a modern edition of the Fasciculus Chemicus, a 17th-century anthology of alchemical writings put together by Arthur Dee (son of the famous Elizabethan scholar John Dee) and translated into English by Elias Ashmole, discussing what the collection reveals about English alchemical thought at the time.

items.work: Brann, Trithemius and Magical Theology
items.abstract: Brann examines Johannes Trithemius, a Benedictine abbot who tried to argue that certain forms of magic were compatible with, and even useful to, Christian faith, and shows how this claim triggered a long-running controversy over the occult that ran through the Reformation and into the rise of modern science.

items.work: Charles Zika, 'Cannibalism and Witchcraft in Early Modern Europe'
items.abstract: Zika looks at prints and paintings from early modern Europe that show witches supposedly cooking and eating babies, arguing that these shocking images reflected deep social anxieties about community breakdown and moral corruption rather than any real evidence that such cannibalism happened.

items.work: Edward Peters, The Magician, the Witch, and the Law
items.abstract: Peters traces how medieval Europe's view of magic changed over time: early on, magic was treated as a learnable (if suspect) skill, but law and church authorities gradually redefined it as heresy, turning practitioners once seen as skilled specialists into criminals who could be prosecuted as witches.

items.work: Ginzburg, The Cheese and the Worms
items.abstract: Ginzburg reconstructs the strange private worldview of Menocchio, a 16th-century Italian miller put on trial by the Inquisition for claiming the universe formed the way cheese curdles, with worms (which he equated with angels) appearing inside it, using the case to explore how ordinary people quietly reworked religious ideas in their own terms.

items.work: Hanegraaff et al., Hermes Explains
items.abstract: Thirty scholars each answer a basic question about "Western esotericism" -- the umbrella term for alternative religious and philosophical traditions like alchemy, astrology, and magic -- aiming to explain the field in plain terms to readers who have never studied it before.

items.work: Johannes Reuchlin, Augenspiegel
items.abstract: In 1511 the German scholar Johannes Reuchlin published this pamphlet to argue against a campaign to burn all Jewish religious books, risking his own reputation to defend Jewish scholarship. This Brill edition is the first full modern English translation, with notes explaining the controversy and why it mattered for early debates about tolerance and free inquiry.

items.work: M. David Litwa, Hermetica II
items.abstract: Litwa translates a scattered set of ancient writings credited to Hermes Trismegistus, a legendary wise teacher who blended Egyptian and Greek ideas about God and the cosmos. The book rounds out an earlier translated collection of these "Hermetic" texts, which ancient readers used for spiritual philosophy and which later Renaissance magicians mined for ideas.

items.work: Marsilio Ficino, De vita coelitus comparanda
items.abstract: This is the third of three short books the philosopher Marsilio Ficino wrote in the 1480s on staying healthy; in it he explains how he believed a scholar could capture beneficial influences from the stars and planets through talismans, music, and diet to fight off the sluggishness and illness he blamed on too much studying. This University of Chicago Press-distributed edition prints the original Latin alongside a full English translation.

items.work: Mebane, Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age
items.abstract: Mebane argues that Renaissance ideas about magic - the belief that a learned person could unlock hidden powers in nature - shaped major English plays like Doctor Faustus, The Alchemist, and The Tempest, and even fed contemporary hopes for moral and scientific progress.

items.work: Ovanes Akopyan, Debating the Stars in the Italian Renaissance
items.abstract: Akopyan traces the argument that broke out after the philosopher Pico della Mirandola wrote a book in the 1490s attacking astrology, the belief that the positions of stars and planets control events on Earth. The book follows how supporters and critics across Italy and Europe fought over Pico's ideas for decades after his death.

items.work: Owen Davies, Grimoires: A History of Magic Books
items.abstract: Davies traces the history of "grimoires," handbooks of magic spells, charms, and rituals, from the ancient Middle East to modern America. He shows that these books, often imagined as purely forbidden or sinister, actually helped spread literacy, religious ideas, and even early science over two thousand years.

items.work: Petropoulos, Greek Magic: Ancient, Medieval and Modern
items.abstract: A collection of essays by different scholars tracing how Greek magic, including spells, curses, and protective charms, changed and stayed the same across three time periods: ancient Greece, the medieval Byzantine world, and modern Greek folk practice.

items.work: Religion, Magic, and the Origins of Science in Early Modern England
items.abstract: John Henry collects his essays making the case that in England between roughly 1500 and 1700, religious belief and older ideas about magic were not obstacles to the rise of modern science. Instead, he argues, they often supplied the very motivations and mental tools early scientists used to investigate nature.

items.work: Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements in the Graeco-Egyptian Magical Papyri
items.abstract: Skinner works through surviving magical papyrus scrolls from Roman-era Egypt to catalog the actual tools and step-by-step methods ancient magicians used, such as bowls and lamps for seeing visions, potions, and ritual objects, and traces how these methods were passed down into later European magic books.

items.work: Stephen A. Farmer, Giovanni Pico della Mirandola: Syncretism in the West
items.abstract: This book presents a full translation and study of Pico della Mirandola's famous 900 Theses of 1486, in which the young philosopher tried to show that Christian, Jewish, Islamic, and ancient Greek and Roman ideas about the universe could all be combined into one grand system of thought.

items.work: Walter Scott, Letters on Demonology and Witchcraft
items.abstract: Novelist Sir Walter Scott's 1830 book, written as a series of letters to a friend, surveys centuries of ghost stories, witch trials, and demon lore. Scott argues that most reported supernatural events had ordinary explanations, such as fear, trickery, or mental illness, making this one of the first attempts to study magic and witchcraft in a rational, skeptical way.

groups.title: Alchemy scholarship
groups.sub: Scholarship on alchemy, incl. Lyndy Abraham's Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery

items.work: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery
items.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

items.work: Lawrence M. Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
items.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

items.work: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Hermetic Spirituality and the Historical Imagination
items.abstract: Looks at the ancient Egyptian writings attributed to the legendary teacher Hermes Trismegistus and argues they weren't just abstract philosophy — they describe real spiritual exercises meant to shift a person's state of mind so they could see past ordinary illusions to a deeper truth about reality.

items.work: Lawrence Principe & William Newman, 'Some Problems with the Historiography of Alchemy'
items.abstract: A widely cited essay (a chapter in the book Secrets of Nature) arguing that many 20th-century scholars misread old alchemy as secret spiritual symbolism, when most alchemists were actually doing hands-on laboratory work aimed at real, physical transformations of matter.

items.work: Leah DeVun, Prophecy, Alchemy, and the End of Time: John of Rupescissa
items.abstract: Tells the story of a 14th-century friar, John of Rupescissa, who believed the end of the world was near and argued that alchemy could produce medicines and materials to help people survive the coming disasters.

items.work: McLean, A Commentary on the Mutus Liber
items.abstract: A plate-by-plate guide to the Mutus Liber ("Wordless Book"), a strange 17th-century alchemy book made almost entirely of pictures with no text, walking through what each mysterious engraving might mean.

items.work: Greco-Egyptian alchemy
items.abstract: Traces how alchemy first took shape in ancient Alexandria, Egypt, where Greek philosophical ideas blended with hands-on Egyptian craft knowledge like metalworking and dyeing, forming the roots of what would eventually become chemistry.

items.work: Michael Maier, Atalanta Fugiens
items.abstract: A 1617 book of 50 alchemical emblems, each combining a strange allegorical picture, a short poem, and a piece of music, that uses the Greek myth of the racing huntress Atalanta as a coded map of the alchemical process.

items.work: Stanton J. Linden (ed.), The Alchemy Reader: From Hermes Trismegistus to Isaac Newton
items.abstract: A collected anthology of alchemy writings in English translation, running from the legendary founder-figure Hermes Trismegistus in antiquity all the way to Isaac Newton in the 1600s, showing how ideas about transforming matter changed over two thousand years.

items.work: Tara Nummedal, 'Words and Works in the History of Alchemy'
items.abstract: Argues that historians should study alchemy not just as ideas on a page but as something people actually did with their hands, reading, writing, and running experiments all at once in settings ranging from royal courts to artisan workshops.

items.work: William Newman, 'Thomas Vaughan as an Interpreter of Agrippa'
items.abstract: Examines how the 17th-century alchemist Thomas Vaughan read and used the earlier writer Agrippa's ideas, arguing Vaughan understood alchemy as involving real physical processes rather than purely spiritual metaphor.

items.work: William R. Newman, Newton the Alchemist
items.abstract: Shows that Isaac Newton, famous for physics and math, also spent decades secretly doing alchemy experiments, and uses Newton's own lab notes, plus modern recreations of his experiments, to figure out what he was actually trying to achieve.

items.work: William R. Newman, on the Latin Geber
items.abstract: Investigates who really wrote the influential medieval Latin alchemy texts credited to "Geber" (supposedly the earlier Arabic alchemist Jabir ibn Hayyan), concluding the real author was most likely a European friar writing under that famous borrowed name.

items.work: William R. Newman, The Summa Perfectionis of Pseudo-Geber
items.abstract: A full scholarly edition and translation of the Summa Perfectionis, the most influential alchemy textbook of the Middle Ages, paired with a study of who actually wrote it and how it shaped centuries of later alchemical thought.

groups.title: Etymological & lexical references
groups.sub: Etymonline, the OED, and the classical lexica

items.work: Etymonline (Douglas Harper)
Bosworth–Toller, An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary
Liddell–Scott–Jones, A Greek–English Lexicon

groups.title: Further reference
groups.sub: Standard scholarly works consulted alongside the corpus

items.work: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
items.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

items.work: S. J. Tambiah, 'The Magical Power of Words'
items.abstract: Looking closely at a Sri Lankan exorcism ceremony, anthropologist Tambiah argues that spells and magic words "work" not because they're bad science but because of how ritual speech is performed - the repetition, rhythm, and formula make the words feel powerful, the same way a wedding vow or courtroom oath does real social work just by being spoken aloud.

items.work: Bull, The Tradition of Hermes Trismegistus
items.abstract: This book argues that the mystical writings credited to "Hermes Trismegistus" (a legendary wise teacher blending the Greek god Hermes with the Egyptian god Thoth) weren't just abstract philosophy, but grew out of real spiritual exercises and rituals practiced by small circles of Egyptian priests who combined their temple traditions with Greek philosophy.

items.work: Claire Fanger (ed.), Conjuring Spirits: Texts and Traditions of Medieval Ritual Magic
items.abstract: A collection of scholarly essays examining actual medieval manuscripts used for ritual magic - instructions for summoning angels or demons, memory-boosting rites, and divination texts - to show how educated Christians in the Middle Ages practiced and argued about magic.

items.work: D. P. Walker, Spiritual and Demonic Magic from Ficino to Campanella
items.abstract: Walker traces how Renaissance thinkers, from the philosopher Marsilio Ficino to the friar Tommaso Campanella, tried to draw a line between magic they considered natural and harmless and magic they worried actually called on demons, showing how these debates were tangled up with ideas about religion, music, and medicine.

items.work: Don C. Skemer, Binding Words
items.abstract: Skemer studies small handwritten charms called textual amulets - strips of parchment inscribed with prayers or words of power that medieval people wore on their bodies or carried for protection - showing how these objects blurred the line between everyday religious devotion and magic.

items.work: H. W. Parke, Sibyls and Sibylline Prophecy in Classical Antiquity
items.abstract: Parke traces the legendary Sibyls -- prophetic women in the ancient Mediterranean believed to deliver cryptic verse prophecies -- from their earliest appearances in Greek sources through their adoption by the Romans and, later, early Christians who reinterpreted their sayings as predictions of Christ.

items.work: Moshe Sluhovsky, Believe Not Every Spirit
items.abstract: This book explores why so many women in early modern Europe were accused of demonic possession, and how Catholic churchmen developed methods for judging whether a person's visions or trances came from God, from the devil, or were simply faked.

items.work: Nancy Caciola, Discerning Spirits
items.abstract: Caciola traces medieval methods for telling whether someone who seemed "possessed" was inhabited by a holy spirit, an evil one, or was just sick, showing that these judgments depended as much on the possessed person's gender and social standing as on religious doctrine.

items.work: Alice B. Kehoe, Shamans and Religion
items.abstract: Kehoe argues that the word "shaman," which comes from a specific Siberian people, has been stretched by Western writers to describe almost any spiritual healer worldwide, and that this loose, romanticized use of the term reveals more about Western fantasies of "primitive" cultures than about the real practices it claims to name.

items.work: Alison Winter, Mesmerized: Powers of Mind in Victorian Britain
items.abstract: Winter tells the story of "mesmerism," an early form of hypnosis said to work through an invisible magnetic fluid, showing that in Victorian Britain it wasn't a fringe curiosity but a widely debated practice that doctors, scientists, and ordinary people took seriously while arguing over where real science ended and quackery began.

items.work: Andrei Znamenski, The Beauty of the Primitive
items.abstract: This book traces how "shamanism," a word first used by explorers to describe Siberian religious specialists, was picked up by generations of Western writers, scholars, and spiritual seekers and reshaped into a symbol of nature-loving, anti-modern spirituality, often saying more about Western longings than about the Siberian traditions it was named after.

items.work: Antoine Faivre, Access to Western Esotericism
items.abstract: Faivre, one of the founders of the academic study of Western esotericism (hidden or symbolic spiritual traditions like alchemy and astrology), gives a beginner-friendly overview tracing these currents of thought from ancient times through the Renaissance up to the present day.

items.work: Artemidorus, Oneirocritica
items.abstract: Written in the second century CE, this is the oldest surviving guide to interpreting dreams, cataloguing thousands of dream images and explaining what each was believed to mean for the dreamer's real life, treating dream interpretation as a practical skill much like fortune-telling.

items.work: Benedek Láng, Unlocked Books
items.abstract: Láng examines which handwritten books about magic - astrology, alchemy, and spirit-summoning texts - actually sat on the shelves of monasteries, royal courts, and universities in medieval Central Europe, showing that this kind of "learned magic" was a normal, if sometimes risky, part of intellectual life rather than something practiced only in secret.

items.work: Brian Copenhaver, Magic and the Dignity of Man
items.abstract: Copenhaver challenges the popular idea that Renaissance philosopher Pico della Mirandola's famous speech was a humanist manifesto about human free will and dignity; he argues the text was really about magic and Jewish mysticism (Kabbalah), and that its modern reputation as being about "the dignity of man" is a five-century-long case of mistaken memory.

items.work: Christopher A. Faraone, Ancient Greek Love Magic
items.abstract: Faraone studies real spells, potions, and charms that ancient Greeks used to make someone fall in love, stay faithful, or come back to them, and shows that these love-magic recipes worked very differently depending on whether a man or a woman was doing the casting.

items.work: Christopher A. Faraone, on binding figurines
items.abstract: This 1991 journal article examines small clay or wax dolls that ancient Greeks bound with cords and buried in the ground to trap a dangerous spirit or curse a rival, arguing that these "voodoo dolls" were used mainly to hold a threat in place rather than to destroy it.

items.work: Cicero, De Divinatione
items.abstract: In this ancient dialogue, the Roman statesman Cicero has two speakers argue over whether divination -- predicting the future through omens, dreams, and oracles -- is a genuine skill or just wishful superstition, ultimately siding with reason and skepticism over popular belief.

items.work: Colin H. Roberts & T. C. Skeat, The Birth of the Codex
items.abstract: This short study explains how the modern book format -- separate pages bound together, called a codex -- replaced the ancient scroll, and argues that early Christians were the ones who most enthusiastically adopted this new, more practical way of storing text.

items.work: Cornelius Agrippa, De Occulta Philosophia
items.abstract: Written in the early 1500s, this is Renaissance scholar Cornelius Agrippa's sweeping handbook of magic, describing three connected levels of hidden power -- in nature, in the stars, and in the divine -- and explaining how a learned magician could try to tap into each one; this edition is the standard modern scholarly edition of the original Latin text.

items.work: Daniel Ogden, Witchcraft and Magic in Europe: Greece and Rome
items.abstract: This collection gathers essays by several scholars on magic in the ancient Greek and Roman world, including Daniel Ogden's chapter on curse tablets and "voodoo dolls" used to bind or harm enemies, alongside chapters on witches in literature and how early Christians later recast old pagan magic as demonic.

items.work: Eamon Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars
items.abstract: Duffy argues that ordinary people in England deeply loved and actively took part in traditional Catholic religious life right up until the Protestant Reformation tore it down, pushing back against the older idea that the medieval church was corrupt and already unpopular before reformers arrived.

items.work: Georg Luck, Arcana Mundi
items.abstract: This book collects and translates ancient Greek and Roman writings about magic, witches, and the occult, giving readers direct access to primary sources that show how ancient people actually talked about spells, curses, and supernatural power.

items.work: Geraldine Pinch, Magic in Ancient Egypt
items.abstract: Pinch surveys how ancient Egyptians used magic in everyday life, from protective amulets worn by pregnant women to spells recited by priests, showing that magic, religion, and medicine were not separate categories for the Egyptians but worked together as one connected system.

items.work: Jean Bottéro, Mesopotamia
items.abstract: This is a collection of essays by French scholar Jean Bottéro (published as 'Mesopotamia: Writing, Reasoning, and the Gods') about the ancient civilizations of Mesopotamia, the region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers in what is now Iraq. It covers how writing was invented there and how people practiced divination — reading signs like the shapes of animal organs to learn the will of the gods — arguing this careful, rule-based thinking was an early ancestor of scientific reasoning.

items.work: Jean MacIntosh Turfa, Divining the Etruscan World
items.abstract: This book gives the first complete English translation of the 'Brontoscopic Calendar,' an ancient text that told the Etruscans (a people who lived in what is now Italy before the Romans) what different days' thunder was supposed to mean for the future. It explains how this calendar fit into Etruscan religious life and traces its roots back to even older Mesopotamian methods of predicting events from natural signs.

items.work: Jessie Weston, From Ritual to Romance
items.abstract: This early-1900s study argues that the medieval legends about the search for the Holy Grail (the sacred cup in King Arthur stories) did not start as purely Christian tales, but grew out of much older fertility rituals — ceremonies meant to keep crops, animals, and land healthy and productive. The book later became famous for directly inspiring T. S. Eliot's poem 'The Waste Land.'

items.work: John J. Collins, on the Sibylline Oracles
items.abstract: Based on his Harvard doctoral research, John J. Collins's 'The Sibylline Oracles of Egyptian Judaism' studies a set of ancient prophetic poems credited to the 'Sibyl,' a legendary Greek prophetess. Collins shows these particular oracles were actually composed by Jewish writers living in Egypt, who borrowed a popular pagan literary style to spread their own religious and political messages.

items.work: Leigh Eric Schmidt, 'The Making of Modern Mysticism'
items.abstract: This journal article argues that today's popular idea of 'mysticism' — an intensely personal, direct spiritual experience separate from organized religion — is not an ancient concept but a fairly modern invention. Schmidt traces how it first took shape in English Enlightenment-era religious arguments and was then reshaped again by 19th-century American Transcendentalist thinkers.

items.work: Lynn Thorndike, A History of Magic and Experimental Science
items.abstract: This massive eight-volume history, published between 1923 and 1958, traces how magic and science developed together in Europe from the early Christian centuries through the 1600s. Thorndike's central argument is that early scientific thinking did not spring up in opposition to magic and the occult, but actually grew out of the same beliefs and practices.

items.work: Mastrocinque et al., Ancient Magic: Then and Now
items.abstract: This is a collection of essays by classical-studies scholars defending 'magic' as a useful, meaningful category for understanding the ancient world, rather than a vague or biased label. Contributors offer fresh interpretations of real magical objects from Greek and Roman antiquity, such as curse tablets, protective amulets, and written spell recipes.

items.work: Michael D. Bailey, Magic and Superstition in Europe
items.abstract: This single-volume overview traces how Europeans have understood, believed in, feared, and punished 'magic' and 'superstition,' from the ancient Near East and Greco-Roman world through the medieval and early modern witch trials, up to the spread of modern witchcraft movements like Wicca into the United States.

items.work: Michel de Certeau, The Mystic Fable
items.abstract: This book examines Christian mysticism — the belief in a direct, personal encounter with God — as it appeared in Europe during the 1500s and 1600s. De Certeau treats mystical writing as a distinctive kind of language that emerged at a moment when older, shared religious certainties were starting to break down.

items.work: Michel Foucault, The Care of the Self
items.abstract: The third volume of Foucault's History of Sexuality looks at how Greek and Roman writers in the first two centuries CE thought about pleasure, self-discipline, and bodily health. Foucault argues that during this period attitudes shifted toward more caution and anxiety about sex, compared with earlier classical Greek views.

items.work: Mircea Eliade, Patterns in Comparative Religion
items.abstract: This wide-ranging 1958 survey compares religious symbols and practices from many different cultures and time periods — things like sky gods, sacred stones, the moon, and fertility rites — to argue that similar patterns of 'the sacred' (what people treat as set apart and holy) show up again and again across human history.

items.work: Nicolas Weill-Parot, on astrology and occult knowledge
items.abstract: This detailed study (published as 'Les images astrologiques au Moyen Age et à la Renaissance') traces how medieval and Renaissance scholars tried to justify 'astrological images' — talismans believed to draw power down from the stars — as a form of natural science rather than forbidden magic. It shows how this argument shaped bigger debates about nature, technology, and the line between science and the occult.

items.work: Ronald Hutton, Blood and Mistletoe: The History of the Druids in Britain
items.abstract: Because almost nothing reliable actually survives about the ancient Druids (priests of pre-Roman Britain), this book instead tells the story of how later generations of British writers, antiquarians, and Romantic-era enthusiasts imagined and reinvented the Druids to fit their own changing ideas, across roughly two thousand years.

items.work: Ronald Hutton, on modern paganism and the runic revival
items.abstract: This refers to historian Ronald Hutton's broader research into how modern Pagan and occult movements, starting in the 1800s, revived old Germanic and Norse rune alphabets (originally just letters used for writing) as tools for magic, divination, and national identity. No single, specific title matching this exact description could be verified against a real public webpage, so no link is given here.

items.work: Ronald Hutton, Shamans: Siberian Spirituality and the Western Imagination
items.abstract: This book traces how Western writers and travelers, from medieval times through the Soviet era, imagined and often misunderstood the shamans (spiritual healers and mediums) of Siberia, and how those imagined images later shaped modern Western ideas about shamanism as a spiritual practice.

items.work: S. R. F. Price, 'The Future of Dreams: From Freud to Artemidorus'
items.abstract: This article compares how the ancient Greek dream-interpretation manual by Artemidorus explained dreams to how Sigmund Freud later explained them, arguing that ancient dream interpreters were doing something different from modern psychology rather than a cruder version of the same thing.

items.work: Steven Katz, Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis
items.abstract: This edited collection of essays argues that mystical experiences (intense, direct feelings of contact with the divine or ultimate reality) are shaped by the beliefs and culture of the person having them, meaning a Christian mystic and a Buddhist mystic do not actually have the identical experience described in different words.

items.work: Stuart McKie, Living and Cursing in the Roman West
items.abstract: Drawing on more than 600 ancient Roman curse tablets (small inscribed sheets of metal asking the gods to harm a rival or thief), this book looks at the everyday lives and social relationships of the ordinary people who wrote and buried them, treating cursing as a normal part of daily religious life rather than something strange or marginal.

items.work: Tamsyn Barton, Ancient Astrology
items.abstract: This book explains how astrology (the belief that the positions of stars and planets influence human events) developed in Mesopotamia and spread through Greece and Rome, and examines the social role astrologers played and why the practice appealed to people at every level of ancient society.

items.work: William Pietz, 'The Problem of the Fetish'
items.abstract: This is the first installment of a three-part study tracing how the idea of a 'fetish' (an object believed to hold magical power) was invented by European traders describing West African religious objects in the 1500s-1600s, and how that idea later got applied, often unfairly, to describe other cultures' beliefs.

items.work: Wouter Hanegraaff, Peter Forshaw & Marco Pasi (eds.), Hermes Explains
items.abstract: This is a collection of short, plain-language answers by leading scholars to thirty basic questions about Western esotericism (a broad label for historical traditions like alchemy, astrology, and magic that mainstream religion and science pushed to the margins), aimed at readers with no prior background in the subject.

note: Aggregated from the sources cited on the term pages and Word Histories. Databases collapse their many s.v. citations into one entry.
